description: 30-slide DALP deck on composable token design and configurable compliance

slides.title: DALP Token Design Controls
slides.subtitle: Composable tokens and configurable compliance | March 2026

slides.title: Minting is not enough
slides.body:
• Tokenization in production includes issuance, compliance, custody coordination, settlement, servicing, and redemption, not only minting.
• Institutions need an operating model that connects product, risk, operations, and technology teams around the same asset record.
• Fragmented point solutions create manual review, reconciliation risk, and weak evidence trails once a pilot goes live.
• DALP is positioned around the full lifecycle so teams can move from pilot to production without re-architecting.

slides.title: What institutions must control
slides.item1:
Investor eligibility
Who can subscribe, hold, and transfer cannot be left to off-chain checklists once regulated assets move into production.
slides.item2:
Transfer permissions
Jurisdiction, restriction, holding, and supply conditions need enforcement before the asset state changes on-chain.
slides.item3:
Servicing events
Coupons, redemptions, distributions, and corporate actions require repeatable workflows after issuance, not ad hoc spreadsheets.
slides.item4:
Operational evidence
Approvals, audit trails, monitoring, and integration outputs must support internal governance and external review.

slides.title: Basic token vs DALP
table_data: Use governed asset classes and templates

slides.title: What composable means
slides.body:
• In DALP terms, a token is not treated as a single fixed contract template with hard-coded behavior for every use case.
• DALP models assets through configurable building blocks: asset class flows, metadata, supply rules, permissions, identity requirements, and compliance modules.
• That lets teams start from pre-built asset classes such as bonds, equity, funds, deposits, stablecoins, precious metals, and real estate.
• The platform can then adapt the operating logic without forcing a full rebuild for each new product.

slides.title: Configurable token building blocks
slides.item1:
Asset class
Choose a governed starting point for the product.
slides.item2:
Metadata
Define fields that shape the instrument record.
slides.item3:
Supply rules
Set issuance windows, caps, and controlled expansion logic.
slides.item4:
Permissions
Assign roles and execution rights by workflow.
slides.item5:
Compliance
Attach policy modules and transfer conditions.
slides.item6:
Identity
Require verified claims before distribution and transfer.

slides.title: Composable token model
slides.body: This Mermaid view shows the DALP asset model as a governed composition problem. A supported asset class becomes the anchor, then terms, metadata, supply controls, permissions, compliance modules, and identity requirements are added as configurable layers. The result is not a generic token with manual overlays; it is a product definition that can be reviewed, approved, launched, and reused.

slides.title: Asset design in DALP
slides.body: The DALP asset designer shows how product teams move through structured setup instead of free-form contract work. Screens in the platform expose instrument details, pricing inputs, compliance modules, permissions, and final review. That matters because the product design, control model, and deployment workflow stay connected in one governed process.
slides.image_path: templates/dalp-screenshots/03 - Asset Designer/Asset Designer - Step 6 - Compliance Modules.jpg

slides.title: Why composability matters
slides.col1:
Reuse across assets
• Start from supported asset classes.
• Avoid redesign for every launch.
• Extend one platform across programs.
slides.col2:
Faster approvals
• Make controls visible during design.
• Review policy choices before issuance.
• Reduce last-minute exceptions.
slides.col3:
Cleaner integration
• Keep APIs, events, and workflows aligned.
• Connect custody and core systems.
• Preserve one operating model.

slides.title: Compliance is ex ante
slides.body:
• DALP enforces compliance before execution, so blocked transfers fail before the token state changes.
• Identity is anchored through OnchainID and reusable verified claims rather than wallet addresses alone.
• Policies can model jurisdiction, eligibility, holding periods, supply limits, and transfer conditions without rewriting the whole platform.
• That lets legal, compliance, and operations teams configure controls close to the product workflow instead of bolting them on later.

slides.title: Policy templates in DALP
slides.body: Policy templates make compliance reusable. Teams can create, review, and apply predefined rules instead of rebuilding control logic for each program. In practice, that supports faster product launches, clearer governance, and more consistent transfer behavior across asset classes and jurisdictions.
slides.image_path: templates/dalp-screenshots/11 - Compliance and Policy Templates/Policy Templates - List.jpg

slides.title: Identity gates distribution
slides.body: Verified identity is part of the token operating model, not a separate filing cabinet. DALP links identities and claims to transfer eligibility so institutions can control who may receive, hold, and move an instrument. That is central for regulated distribution, onboarding reuse, and auditable policy enforcement.

slides.title: Compliance control families
slides.item1:
Policy templates
Standardize controls for common products and jurisdictions so teams launch faster without losing review discipline.
slides.item2:
Transfer restrictions
Evaluate jurisdiction, holder status, supply, or holding conditions before execution instead of after the fact.
slides.item3:
Roles and approvals
Use RBAC and maker-checker style controls to separate design, approval, and operational actions.
slides.item4:
Identity checks
Bind verified claims to transaction permissions so eligibility can be reused across workflows.

slides.title: Transfer decision flow
slides.body: This Mermaid diagram illustrates the DALP compliance path around a transfer. The platform looks up the applicable token policy, checks identity and claims, evaluates transfer restrictions, and then either approves execution or blocks the movement before it settles. The important point for institutions is that compliance logic is operationalized inside the platform workflow, with an audit trail after the decision.

slides.title: Lifecycle on one platform
slides.body: DALP is designed around the full digital asset lifecycle. Asset design, issuance setup, onboarding, distribution, transfers, settlement, servicing events, redemption, and reporting are treated as connected operating stages rather than separate tools. That is what makes the platform useful after launch, when control, evidence, and servicing work matter as much as issuance.

slides.title: Operations after issuance
slides.body: Production credibility depends on what happens after the asset goes live. DALP includes monitoring and operational visibility so teams can track requests, activity, and runtime behavior. Combined with auditability, durable workflows, and integration outputs, that helps institutions manage live programs instead of stopping at issuance day.

slides.title: Who owns what
slides.col1:
Design and issuance
• Product defines the asset structure and terms.
• Risk and legal review policy choices.
• Operations prepare onboarding and approvals.
• Technology connects the target systems.
slides.col2:
Servicing and control
• Operations manage transfers and exceptions.
• Treasury or servicing teams run payouts and redemptions.
• Compliance monitors policy outcomes.
• Technology supports observability and integrations.

slides.title: Lifecycle control points
slides.body: Each lifecycle stage has a control objective, a DALP mechanism, and an output that institutions can evidence. The point is not just automation. It is making design, issuance, transfer, servicing, and redemption auditable across the same operating model.

slides.title: Core regulated assets
slides.col1:
Bonds
• Bond is a pre-built asset class.
• Support issuance, settlement, and servicing.
• Keep coupon and maturity logic governed.
slides.col2:
Funds
• Fund workflows support subscriptions and redemptions.
• Reuse investor controls across distribution.
• Connect valuation and servicing processes.
slides.col3:
Equity
• Control who can hold and transfer.
• Keep issuance and servicing aligned.
• Extend with governance-related features.

slides.title: Cash and alternatives
slides.col1:
Deposits
• Supported as a DALP asset class.
• Use permissions and policy logic for issuance.
• Fit institutional cash workflows.
slides.col2:
Stablecoins
• Supported as a DALP asset class.
• Configure supply and transfer controls.
• Operate with identity-aware distribution.
slides.col3:
Precious metals
• Supported as a DALP asset class.
• Link ownership to governed records.
• Apply policy and servicing controls.

slides.title: Real estate programs
slides.body: Real estate shows why composable tokens and configurable compliance belong together. The token is only one part of the operating model. Institutions also need verified ownership, transfer restrictions, approvals, servicing events, and integration with external records. DALP positions real estate as a governed asset class rather than an ad hoc smart-contract project.
slides.image_path: templates/dalp-screenshots/10 - Real Estate/Real Estate - Doha Business Towers - Asset Details.jpg

slides.title: Why institutions choose DALP
slides.item1:
Platformized model
DALP covers issuance, compliance, settlement, servicing, and reporting in one operating model instead of siloed point tools.
slides.item2:
Configurable controls
Teams combine asset classes, policies, permissions, and identity checks without rebuilding the stack for each use case.
slides.item3:
Compliance first
RBAC, approvals, identity-linked rules, and ex-ante transfer checks fit regulated institutions better than generic token tooling.
slides.item4:
Production ready
APIs, webhooks, custody integration, observability, and deployment flexibility support live institutional programs.
